text: BOROVITCHI, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 97 m. ESE of Novgorod, on the r. bank of the Msta. Pop. 4,000. It contains 4 churches and a convent, and has a considerable trade. In the vicinity are the Borovitskie porogui or rapids, upwards of 21 m. in extent. They have been rendered navigable. The dist. is hilly, and contains limestone, coal, and some saline springs. Pop. 64,183.

BOROVSK, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 50 m. NNE of Kalonga, on the r. bank of the Provta, 60 m. SW of Moscow. Pop. 6,000. It contains 4 churches, and has manufactories of sail-cloth. Lint, hemp, and leather, form its chief articles of trade. This town gives its name to the eparchy or episcopal diocese of Kalonga and Barovsk, and is celebrated as the scene of the valorous defence made by Prince Michel Volkonsky against the troops of the false Demitri in 1610. In the environs is the convent of Pavnoutief-Borovskii, founded in 1444, and reputed one of the richest in the empire. The district is watered by the Provta and Louja, and is to a great extent covered with forests. It is famous for its garlic and onions, and has extensive manufactories of linen and stockings. Pop. 56,474.
BOROW, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Lublin and obwod of Zamosz, famous for the battle between the Russians and Poles, which took place here in Sept., 1831.
BOROWA, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 27 m. SE of Craslau, and 40 m. S of Koniggratz. It consists of about 122 houses.
BOROWNO, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Kaliscz, in the obwod and 45 m. from Wielun. Pop. 480.
BOROX, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 20 m. NE of Toledo.
BOROZDA. See TEREK.
BOROZDENKI, a village of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 35 m. NE of Toula, in the dist. and 11 m. N of Venev. Pop. 1,200.
BOROZDINSK, a fort of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Caucasus, in the dist. and 10 m. W of Kizliar, near the N bank of the Terek. It was built in 1735, and is occupied by the Cossacks.
BORRACHA ISLAND, an island of the Carib- | Pop. in 1831, 1,185; in 1841, 1,625. bean sea, 10 m. N of Barcelona, in N lat. 10° 20', and W long. 64° 48'.
BORRACHOS POINT, a headland of the state of Ecuador, in S lat. 0° 20'.

BORRACHUDO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, comarca of Sabara, which takes its rise in the Sierra Mattagorda, runs NE, and unites with the St. Francisco at the town of S. Felix. BOR BORRADAILES, Komp, or Fish River, a river of Southern Africa, in Great Namaqualand, formed by the union of several streams, in S lat. 27° 57', and which, after a course of 40 m. to the S, falls into Orange river, 50 m. above its entrance into the Atlantic.
BORRAS-HOVAH, a township in the p. of Wrexham, Denbighshire, 23 m. W by S of Holt. Pop. 43.
BORRAS RIFFRE, a township in the p. of Gresford, Denbighshire, 3 m. NNE of Wrexham. Pop. 37.
BORREGAARD, a town of Denmark, in the prov. of Jutland, in the amt and 30 m. SW of Aalborg, near the E bank of Lymfiord, and 10 m. S of Logstor.
BORRENES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 66 m. W of Leon, and 8 m. SSE of Villafranca.
BORREV, a town of Transylvania, on the r. bank of the Aranyos, 9 m. WSW of Thorda, and 18 m. S of Klausenburg.
BORREZE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Salignac. Pop. 1,049.
BORRIGAL. See PUERTO-RICO.
BORRIOL, or Borrial, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 6 m. NE of Castellon-de-la- Plana. Pop. 3,000.

BORRIS, a rivulet of co. Carlow. It rises in the Scallagh-pass, on the boundary with Wexford, and runs 6 or 7 m. in the direction of W by N, past the village of Borris, to the Barrow, 21 m. below Goresbridge.

BORRIS, or Borris-Idrone, a village in the p. of Clonagoose, co. Carlow, on the Borris rivulet, 14 m. N of New Ross, and 14 m. S of Carlow. It consists of a single row of houses, nearly a mile in length. Area 29 acres. Pop. in 1831, 671; in 1841, 950.—Borris castle, adjoining the above village, is, in every respect, the most imposing and interesting mansion in the county of Carlow. The original edifice was a large quadrangular, unornamented pile; and the present structure was, in the course of last century, altered from it, and constructed in the English baronial mansion style of the 16th cent., turreted, rich in detail, and similar in character to the castles of Hatfield and Burleigh.

BORRIS-IN-OSSORY, a small market and post town, in the p. of Aghaboe, Queen's co., on the great road from Dublin to Limerick, 5\frac{3}{4} m. E of Rosscrea. The village consists of a long street, has a mean appearance, and is maintained by its market, its fairs, a small retail trade, and the ordinary arts of handicraft. Area of the v. 50 acres. Pop. in 1831, 770; in 1841, 821.

BORRISLEAGH, or Two-Mile-Borris, a parish 3½ m. SE of Thurles, co. Tipperary. It contains the villages of Borrisleagh and Littleton. Area 11,939 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,244; in 1841, 3,372. Pop. of the v. of B. 122,
BORRISMORE, a parish in the NW corner of co. Kilkenny, 12 m. E of Urlingford. Area 1,271 acres. Pop. 267.

BORRIS-O'-KANE, a parish in co. Tipperary. It contains the t. of Borris-o'-Kane, and the v. of Goatstown. Area 5,128 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,634; in 1841, 3,175. Houses 538. The town of B. stands on the road from Birr to Nenagh, 11 m. SW of Birr.
BORRIS-O'-LEAGH, a small town in the p. of Glankeen, co. Tipperary, 5 m. SW by S of Templemore. Pop. in 1831, 1,304; in 1841, 1,438.

BORROMEAN ISLES, a group of islets in the Lago Maggiore, stretching along the entrance of the gulf of Tosa, in the prov. of Pallanza, the principal of which is Isola Madre, about 3 m. in circumf., the most northerly of the group. It is covered with , , , , , , , , , , , , , , , , , , , orchards, and orange and citron groves; and contains a fine villa, and a small theatre. Isola Superiore, or Isola-de'-Piscatori, to the SW of the former, contains a small village, inhabited by about 400 fishermen, and a parish-church; and Isola Bella, the most southerly, is adorned with terraced gardens of the richest beauty, and a magnificent palace. These islands, originally arid rocks, owe their present luxuriance to Prince Vitaliano Borromeo, who, in 1671, covered them with soil conveyed from the continent, and in the possession of whose family they have been since the 13th cent.
BORROMEO, or Boromeo, a town of the island of Sicily, in the district of Mazzara, 12 m. SE of Trapani.
BORRONE, an island of the Mediterranean, near the W coast of Sicily, in N lat. 37° 55', E long. 12° 26'.
BORRUP, or Borup, a town of Denmark, in the prov. of Jutland, in the bailiwick and 18 m. WSW of Aalborg.
BORROWBY, a township in the p. of Leak, in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. N of Thirsk. Pop. 401.— Also a township in the p. of Lythe, in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 10 m. NW of Whitby. Pop. 81.

BORROWDALE, a chapelry in the p. of Crossthwaite, Cumberland, 7 m. S by W of Keswick. Pop. in 1801, 342; in 1841, 369. This chapelry is celebrated for the production of the finest plumbago in the world. This mineral—provincially called wad— is found in nodules imbedded in grey felspar porphyry. The vicinity is remarkably picturesque, and is much visited by tourists. At the lower extremity of the dale is a mineral spring somewhat similar to that of Cheltenham.
BORROWDON, a township in the p. of Allenton, Northumberland, 19 m. W by S of Alnwick. Pop. 165.

BORROWSTOUNNESS, or Bo'ness, a parish in the co. of Linlithgow, bounded by the frith of Forth on the N. It is about 4 m. in length, and 2 m. in breadth. The surface declines gradually on the N toward the Forth, and on the W to the river Avon. There are several coal-pits within the parish; ironstone also abounds; and there are beds of limestone, and quarries of freestone and whinstone. Pop. of the town and p. in 1801, 2,790; in 1831, 2,809; in 1841, 2,347.—The burgh of B. is 9 m. W of Queensferry, and 8 m. E of Falkirk, on a low peninsula washed by the Forth, and only a few feet above high water-mark. The streets and lanes are narrow; the houses in general low and old-fashioned. Its pop. in 1831 was 2,188; in 1841, 1,790. There is some ship-building carried on here; and in the immediate vicinity were extensive salt-works, at which 30,000 bushels used to be manufactured annually. The harbour is one of the safest and most commodious in the frith, having a great depth of water. It has some coasting-trade, but its commerce has greatly declined. The harbour has an area of about 1 acre, enclosed by piers, with a scouring basin; it also has a patent slip; and it is of easy access; but it was until lately much out of repair. The expense of an act of parliament in 1844, preparatory to its improvement, amounted to £900, or double the gross annual income of the town and harbour. The arrivals in 1846 were, 719 vessels of 29,663 tons; of these 22 vessels were from the colonies, or foreign, and the customs' duty received, £1,856. The tonnage belonging to the port was 91 vessels of 5,944 tons, the revenue £503, and the debt £2,380. An embankment extends from this place about 12 m. westwards along the N side of the carse of Kinneil. About 50 years ago a canal was begun to be cut between this place and Grangemouth, to communicate there with the Forth and Clyde canal. This canal was never finished. A BOR railway to connect this port with the Slamannan railway has been projected. BORRUCA, BORUCA, OF BURICA POINT, a promontory on the SW coast of Guatemala, in the prov. of Veragua, in N lat. 8° 5', W long. 82° 50'.

BORRUCAS, or Borucas (San Lorenzo de), a town of Guatemala, in the prov. of Costa Rica, on the r. bank of the Dolce, 20 m. above the entrance of that river into the gulf of the same name, and 85 m. SSE of Cartago.

BORSA, a village of Hungary, in the com. of Marmaros, 45 m. SE of Szigeth. Pop. 3,478. It is situated near the source of the Viso, at the base of Mount Szessul, and at the entrance of the gorge of the Tatar-Volgye, which leads into the Bukovine. In the environs are mines of argentiferous lead and of copper, and some foundries.
BORSDORF, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Leipsic, on the Parde river. Pop. 109.

BORSELE, or Monster, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Zeeland, on the SW coast of the island of S Beveland, 9 m. SW of Goes. Pop. 700. It occupies the site of the town of Borse, which was submerged in 1532.

BORSKAIA, a fort of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 175 m. WNW of Orenburg, in the district and 27 m. WNW of Bouzoulouk, on the r. side of the Samara, which is here crossed by a bridge of boats. Pop. 1,500.

BORSOD, or Borssodska, a comitat in the N of Hungary; bounded on the N by the comitats of Gömör and Torna; on the E by those of Zemplin, Abaujvar, and Szabolts; and on the S and W by Heves. It comprises a superficial area of 1,390 sq. m. In 1837 its pop. was estimated at 232,600, chiefly Hungarians, of whom 100,000 were Roman Catholics; 20,000 belonged to the United Greek church; 110,000 to the Reformed; 6,000 were Lutherans; and 8,000 Jews. It is intersected in the N and W by several mountain-ridges; and is watered in its entire extent by the Sayo, which, entering on the NW, unites with the Theiss on its SE confines. Its principal tributary is the Greater Hernad. This district is one of the finest in Hungary; producing grain in large quantities. Its wheat is reputed the best grown in the kingdom; its pastures are also rich; and it produces wine of excellent quality, fruit in great abundance, tobacco, hemp, lint, and timber. Of 512,072 jochs, entire surface, 215,480 are under cultivation; 26,715 consist of prairie and pastureland; 27,994 of vineyards; and the remainder is covered with forests. The forests abound with game, and the rivers with fish. Cattle and fowls are extensively reared, and, in some localities, bees. The mountains, which are outlying declivities of the Carpathian system, contain slate of excellent quality, limestone, and marble. There are also some iron-mines, and mineral springs. Lignite is found in the vicinity of Miskoltz. Cloth and leather form the chief articles of manufacture. The commerce is considerable.- The com. is divided into 4 jaras, or marches, viz., Miskoltz, in which is the cap. of the same name, Erlau, St. Peter, and Szendrö; and comprises 11 towns, and 171 villages. Its contribution to the military chest under the old regime was 63,411 florins.—The name Borsod is derived from an ancient castle, now in ruins.
BORSONY. See PILSEN.
BORSTALL, or BOARSTALL, a parish in Bucks, 6 m. SSE of Bicester. Area 3,080 acres. Pop. 252.
BORSTENDORF, a village of Saxony, on the Erzgebirge, N of Longefeld. Pop. 811. A considerable quantity of turnery-ware and musical instruments is made here.
BORSZCZOW, a seignorial town of Galicia, in the circle and 20 m. SE of Czortkow, and 18 m. NE of Zaleszczyky. Pop. 1,404.

BORSZEG, a village of Transylvania, on the frontier of Moldavia, in the country of the Szeklers, 19 m. N of Gyergyo-Szent-Miklos, and 52 m. ESE of Bistritz. In the neighbourhood are chalybeate springs, and baths which were formerly much frequented. Limestone abounds, and some ancient igneous deposits occur in the environs.

BORT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, arrond. of Ussel. The cant. comprises 10 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 8,675.—The town is on the r. bank of the Chavanoux, 16 m. SE of Ussel, and 40 m. ENE of Tulle, at an alt. of 2,820 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1846, 2,367. It possesses manufactories of linen and cheese; and has an extensive trade in horses, cattle, pigs, deals, sealing-wax, furs, and gloves. In the environs are saw-mills and coal-works; and at Ribeyrolles is a mine of argentiferous lead.—B. was the birth-place of Marmontel.

BORTCHALO, a district of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Georgia, and prov. of Somketia. It comprises the country watered by the Lower Debete, extending from the Inja, by which it is separated from the district of K'azakhi on the W, to the Kur on the E. On the S it is bounded by the district of Pambaki; and on the N by that of Tachiri. Its pop. consists of about 2,500 families, two-thirds of whom are Turcomans, and the remainder Armenians. The latter generally form distinct communities. Agriculture and the rearing of cattle constitute the chief employments. Some mines of copper, iron, and lead, occur in this district.

BORTHWICK, a hilly parish in the S part of Edinburghshire, watered by the South Esk and the Tyne. Pop. in 1801, 842; in 1831, 1,473; in 1841, 1,617. It contains the villages of Clayhouse, Dewarton, Middleton, N Middleton, Newlandrig, and part of Stobbsmills. The fine remains of the old castle of B., erected in 1430, are in this p., 11 m. from Edinburgh, and 1 m. from the Gorebridge station of the Edinburgh and Hawick railway which passes through the p. They occupy a rising ground above the Gore rivulet, a tributary of the Esk.
BORTHWICK WATER, a Roxburghshire stream, whose head-streams are Craighope, Howpassley, and Brownshope burns. It flows NE, and joins the Teviot, a little below Branxholm, after a course of 13 m.
BORTI, a town of Greece, in the prov. of Phthiotis, on a tributary of the Ellada, 40 m. NW of Salona, and about the same distance NNE of Lepanto.
BORTIGALI, a village of the island of Sardinia, in the prov. and 40 m. SE of Sassari, situated on a hill, in a healthy locality, in the vicinity of Mount Santo Padre. Pop. 2,500.
BORTNAS-OE, an island in the North sea, off the W coast of Norway, to the S of the Sogne-fiord, in N lat. 60° 53'.
BORUCA. See BORRUCAS.
BORUNDA, a hamlet of Spain, giving name to a mountain-pass in the Valle de Araquil, in Navarre, the scene of some of the Carlist general Zumalacarregui's most brilliant exploits.
BORUTA, a village of Sardinia, in the district of N lat. 53° 56', and E long. 6° 53'. Sassari, near Monte Santo. Pop. 500.
BORWICK, a chapelry in the p. of Warton, in Lancashire, 31 m. S of Burton-in-Kendal. Area 820 acres. Pop. 214.
BORYSSOW. See Borissor.
BORYSTHENES. See DNIEPER.
BORZAK, or Borsac, an extensive steppe in the Kirghiz-Kassack territory.—Also a sandy plain on the N coast of Lake Aral. BOS
BORZEWO, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Plock, in the obwod and 48 m. from Mlawa. Pop. 250.

BORZNA, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 55 m. ESE of Tchernigov, and 20 m. SSW of Sosnitza. Pop. 900. It contains 6 churches, and has three great annual fairs. The district, watered by a small river of the same name, is generally sandy, but affords pasturage to numerous herds of cattle, and is extensively covered with forests.
BORZONASCA, a town of Sardinia, in the gov. of Genoa, in the prov. and 11 m. N of Chiavari. Pop. 1,500. It has some cloth-manufactories.
BOS, a town of Hungary, on the great Schutt island, 25 m. WNW of Komorn, on a tributary of the Danube.

BOSA, a town in the island of Sardinia, in the prov. and 30 m. S of Sassari, beautifully situated in a fine valley between two tabled hills, on the N bank of the Termo, about 1 m. above its entrance into the gulf of Bosa, and 15 m. WSW of Bonorva. Pop. 6,500. It is built around the base and on the acclivity of a hill on the summit of which is an old ruinous castle, and is encompassed by walls now in a state of dilapidation. It is a bishop's see; and contains one handsome street, a cathedral, and several convents. The site is extremely insalubrious, being a close valley on the muddy banks of a sluggish stream. The port, which is formed by the islet of Rossa, in N lat. 40° 16′ 40″, E long. 8° 25′ 31″ [Smyth], is small, and capable of receiving only small boats; but it is much frequented by the coralfishers of Genoa. Its chief exports are cheese, malvagia, flax, oil, corn, and wine.
BOSA, a town of E Africa, in the Somauli territory, 25 m. N of Hurrur, and 100 m. WSW of Zeila.
BOSBOCH, or ATTARFAIEH, a town of Nubia, in the prov. and 5 m. NNE of Sennaar, on the r. bank of the Nile.
BOSBOS, a town of the island of Gilolo, in the Asiatic archipelago, on a bay of the E coast, in N lat. 1° 10'.
BOSBURY, a parish in Herefordshire, 4 m. N by W of Ledbury. Area 5,200 acres. Pop. 1,137.
BOSCASTLE, a small port of Cornwall, on the Bristol channel, 3 m. SE of Bossiney.
BOSC (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Foix. Pop. 1,890.

BOSCAWEN, a township of New Hampshire, U. S., in Merrimac co., 9 m. NW of Concord. Pop. in 1840, 1,965. It is situated on the W side of the Merrimac, opposite Canterbury, with which it is connected by a bridge; and is intersected by the Blackwater, which affords excellent water-power. It is generally level and very fertile.
BOSCAWEN (CAPE), a promontory at the SW side of the island of Santa Cruz, or Egmont, in Queen Charlotte's group, in S lat. 10° 50', and E long. 165° 40'.
BOSCAWEN'S ISLAND, an island in the S Pacific, one of the Friendly group, in S lat. 16°, and W long. 174°. It is 6 m. in circumf.
BOSCH, an island of Holland, in the North sea, 6 m. from the NW coast of the prov. of Groningen, between the islands of Shiermonik and Rottum, in
BOSCH (HERTOGEN). See Bois-LE-Duc.

BOSCHBERGEN, or Bush Bergen, a range of mountains in S Africa, in the prov. of Somerset, in the territory of the Cape of Good Hope. It extends between Little Fish river on the W, to the Great Fish river on the E, a distance of upwards of 20 m., in S lat. 32° 45'.
BOSCHEN. See BISCHHEIM.

BOSCHI, a town of Piedmont, near the SE confines, on the r. bank of the Aveto, 13 m. S of Bobbio, and 46 m. SE of Alexandria. BOSCHMAN'S or Bushman's River, a river of S Africa, in the territory of the Cape of Good Hope, which takes its rise in the S of Somerset co., separates the co. of Uitenhage and Albany, and falls into the Indian ocean 28 m. S of Graham's Town.
BOSCLARD, or Bosc-le-Hard, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Lower Seine, cant. of Bellencombre, 15 m. NNE of Rouen. Pop. 814.

BOSCO, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, and dist. of Valle-Maggia, 8 m. NNW of Cevio, and 20 m. NNW of Locarno. Pop. 300. It has manufactories of wooden-ware and turnery.- Also a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento of the same name, in the prov. and 7 m. SSE of Alexandria. Pop. 3,000. It has an extensive library, and formerly possessed an abbey.
BOSCOBEL, an extra parochial liberty in the co. of Salop, 6 m. E by N of Shiffnall. Area 700 acres. Pop. 18. Charles I., after his defeat at Worcester, found concealment here for some time.

BOSCOLUNGO, a village of Tuscany, in the district and 40 m. NW of Florence, on a spur of the Apennines, at an alt. of 4,452 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 435. It contains a custom-house, and has some manufactories of wooden-ware.
BOSCOMBE, a parish in Wilts, 4 m. SE of Amesbury. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 156.

BOSCO-TRE-CASE, a village of Naples, in the prov. and 13 m. ESE of Naples, the cap. of a cant. in the dist. of Castel-a-Mare, at the foot of Mount Vesuvius. Pop. 4,500. This is one of 4 villages situated within a short distance of one another, bearing the general name of Bosco. The group contains several parish churches and convents, a royal manufactory of arms, a powder-mill, and an extensive manufactory of maccaroni. The environs afford good wine, and large quantities of silk.
BOSC-ROGER (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Bourgthéroulde. Pop. 2,203.
BOSDARROS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Pyrenees, cant. of Pau. Pop. 1,935.
BOSEMPRA. See Boosempra.
BOSHAM, a parish in Sussex, 3\frac{1}{2} m. W by S of Chichester. Area 3,860 acres. Pop. 1,091.
BOSHAM, or Bocham, a town of Africa, in the kingdom of Gingiro, on the S frontier of Abyssinia, on a tributary of the Zebee, 365 m. SSW of Gondar.
BOSHERSTON, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 53 m. SW of Pembroke. Pop. 225.
BOSINGEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. NNE of Freyburg, near the r. bank of the Saane.
BOSINGFELD, a town of Germany, in the principality of Lippe-Detmold, bailiwick of Sternberg, 10 m. N of Blomberg, and 13 m. NE of Detmold. Pop. 834. It has several fairs.
BOSJARGNA, a town of Sweden, in the district and 150 m. WNW of Pitea, on a branch of the Horn Afvan.
BOSJEAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone and Loire, cant. of St. Germain-du-Bois. Pop. 1,015.
BOSJESMANS. See Bushmen.
BOSKOOP, a town of Holland, in the prov. of S Holland, on the W side of the Aar, 9 m. SE of Leyden, and 13 m. NNE of Rotterdam. Pop. 1,340.

BOSKOWITZ, or Boskowice, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 21 m. NNE of Brunn, and 26 m. WSW of Olmutz. Pop. 4,911, of whom 1,949 are Jews. It contains a fine seignorial castle belonging to the counts of Dietrichstein, founded in 1202, and has a glass-work and some cloth-manufac- BOS tories. The environs contain mines of gold, silver, and alum. Their chief agricultural production is the teasel-plant used by cloth-manufacturers.
BOSLEY, or Bosly, a chapelry in the p. of Prestbury, 41 m. ENE of Congleton. Area 3,180 acres. Pop. 552.

BOSMOREAU, or Bostmoraud, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. and 3 m. N of B., on the r. bank of the Thorion. Pop. 416. It contains coal-mines, the produce of which in 1832, amounted to 1,500 quintals.

BOSNA, a river of European Turkey, in the ejalet of Bosnia. It is composed of numerous small streams, which have their sources on the N declivity of Mount Ivari, on the borders of the Herzegovine, to the SW of Bosna-Serai; and, running from S to N, passes by Vissoko, Maglai, Doboi, and Dobor, and after a course of 110 m. joins the Save 20 m. E of or below Brod. This stream, the largest of all those that water the plain of Serai, is 390 ft. where broadest. Its chief tributaries are the Krivaga and the Jolla on the right, and the Lashva and Ussora on the left. BOSNA-SERAI, SERAJEVO, or SERAIEVO, a city of European Turkey, formerly the cap. of Bosnia, 36 m. SE of Traunik, 46 m. SW of Zwornik, 75 m. SE of Banjaluka, and 495 m. WNW of Constantinople, in 43° 31′ 10" N lat., and 18° 36' E long. It is advantageously situated partly on the plain of the Melaska, a tributary of the Bosna, and partly on the declivities of some small hills. The houses are adorned with gardens; and the place presents a somewhat imposing appearance in its numerous minarets, bastions, and turrets. Several stone bridges establish the communication between the two banks of the river; and the town is defended by a large and strong fortress, consisting of several forts connected together, and each flanked by small towers. Its walls are in a state of dilapidation. There are, it is estimated, in this city 80 mosques, some of which are very elegant, besides several Catholic and Greek churches. The public baths, bazaars, and bezestines, are well furnished.—The pop. has been estimated at 60,000, two thirds of which are Turks, 3,000 Catholics, and the rest Greeks and Jews. The inhabitants are employed in manufacturing arms, hardware, copperutensils, and jewellery, and in making and dressing leather and other fabrics. B. is the centre of commerce with Dalmatia, Croatia, and Southern Germany. The climate is temperate; and, though comparatively cold, the vine flourishes in the vicinity. B. is built upon the ruins of the ancient Tiberiopolis. It was captured by Prince Eugene in 1697, subsequent to the memorable defeat which he gave the Turks at Zenta, but he was not able to gain possession of its citadel. BOSNIA, [Turk. Boshna-Ili,] an ejalet or prov. of European Turkey, which takes its name from the Bosna, one of its principal rivers. It extends between 42° 27' and 45° 17' N lat., and 15° 45' and 19° 2' E long. It is the most western prov. of Turkey; and has on three sides the same limits with that empire. The Save, from the junction of the Verbas, to its confluence with the Drin, separates it from Austria on the N; the Drin, up to the parallel of 44°, separates it on the E from Servia; from this point the E frontier runs along the mountain-ridge which separates the basin of the Upper Drin on the W from that of the Upper Ibar on the E, and skirting the whole W and SW frontier of Servia, strikes the Sibocza river which forms its extreme SE limit. It then turns sharply round and runs NW along the N frontier of Upper Albania and the Herzegovine, until it strikes the Drina in the parallel of 43° 40' N; whence it runs WNW along Mount Plannina, the N frontier of the Herzegovine till it strikes the headstream of the Verbas, which, throughout its whole course to the Save, forms its W boundary, separating it from Croatia. Its mean length is 180 geog. or 207 British miles, from NNW to SSE; and its mean breadth 108 geog. or 125 British m., from ENE to WSW. Its superficies is about 22,000 sq. m. Physical features.] B. is a very mountainous country. The chain of the Dinaric Alps, which enters it at the source of the Unna, under the name of the mountains of Plissivicza and Tepoli, runs from NW to SE almost parallel with the coast of the Adriatic, from which it is never distant above 60 m. This chain forms the division of the waters which run N to the Save, and S to the Adriatic. The principal streams to which it gives birth on the N side, are the Unna, and the Unatz, its SE branch, and the Sanna which joins the Unna at Novi, the Verbas, the Bosna, the Drin, and the Western Morava, between which it throws out very considerable branches. The Narenta is the only remarkable stream, to the S of the range, on which no considerable branches appear, but the Prologh and the Piessiori. Between the first of these branches and the principal chain is the plain of Livno, and a number of irregular valleys. The S branches of the Dinaric Alps have a peculiar character. Far from resembling the N branches, in which are seen an uninterrupted succession of beautiful forests, fine pastures, a vigorous vegetation, and very numerous water courses, these, on the contrary, present, especially near the sea, sharp peaks and rocks bare to the very top. In winter, vegetation is opposed by the impetuous winds; and in summer, by a burning sun, the effect of which is only tempered by the abundant dews. B. abounds in streams. Here the traveller cannot advance half-a-league in any direction without crossing a rivulet. The Drino, on the E boundary of the country, the Bosna watering the central districts, and the Verbas to the W, are all navigable for boats of 50 tons. The Unna, which for 20 m. forms the boundary between Austrian and Turco-Bosnian Croatia, is said by some accounts to be unnavigable from its numerous shallows; though we are told by others that this river has such an abundant supply of water as to be navigable almost to its source. Climate.] The climate of Bosnia is cold, though much varied by local circumstances and the difference of level. On the fruitful plains, near the banks of the Drino, the winter is mild; but severe in Turkish Croatia and the mountainous country, where snow several feet deep covers much of the country for one-half of the year. The centigrade therm. frequently falls here from 12° to 18° below zero. The summer heats are seldom oppressive; but in that season the S parts—as might be expected from the difference of level-are cooler than the N. The forests on the mountains collect the clouds, and the weather is often stormy from June to the middle of August, but the great rains during these months fertilize the ground. Spring, in the high country, begins about the end of April, and continues till June. The heat of summer then commences, and lasts to the beginning of September; snow falls generally before that month is ended, and is not wholly dissolved until the middle of May. B. is on the whole a very healthy country, well supplied with water, and enjoying a salubrious atmosphere. The marshes are almost exclusively confined to the banks of the Save. Productions.] B., consisting of a vast number of narrow valleys, each watered by its own streams, cannot fail of being a fertile region, even in spite of the indolence and barbarism of the natives. The soil in fact yields abundant crops everywhere; but the most fertile districts are those on the banks of the Drino, the Verbagna, and the Korovitza. The strong fort flanked with towers, &c., and furnished valleys and the sides of the hills are covered with a thick stratum of vegetable mould; even the higher summits are not unfruitful. But it appears, upon the whole, that the greater part of the soil is better fitted for pasturage than grain. The forests abound in oaks, ashes, poplars, maples, hornbeams, aspens, and birches. The northern side of the Dinaric Alps, and their branches, are clothed with forests, whilst their summits are covered with thyme, rosemary, and other aromatic plants, interspersed with woods of larches, firs, and yews. A large navy might be built from the timber in these forests; and if proper roads were cut, Bosnian oaks might be transported to the coasts of the Adriatic. The rich meadows and pastures are grazed by numerous herds of oxen. The sheep have fine fleeces. Goats are very numerous. The horses are of middle size, but they are little used except as beasts of burden. Fruits and vegetables are abundant. Pears and apples are remarkable for their size and agreeable taste. Grapes are not-cultivated; but on the banks of the Drin, slivovitza, a kind of strong drink made of plums, is used both by Christians and Turks as a substitute for wine; and all the lands in the vicinity of the villages are, in consequence, planted with plum trees. Pekmes, a sweet juice of the consistence of honey, is extracted from pears. The different kinds of grain raised are wheat, maize, and barley, the produce of which is much above the consumption. Oats are cultivated only in a few places, but millet is a very common crop. In the Herzegovine, or Southern Bosnia, vines and olives are principal productions, especially in the vicinity of Mostar; and tobacco is raised on the banks of the Drin. Minerals.] Iron is the only production which the jealousy of the Turks permits to be drawn from the bowels of the earth; but if B. were well governed, its mines might become a source of wealth. Gold was formerly obtained in this country; and it was probably at Statnitza, 6 m. from Traunik, on the road to Scopia, that the Romans wrought their famous gold mines. According to the traditions of the natives, there are gold mines near Zwornick and Varish; and particles of native gold are rolled down the Bosna, the Verbas, the Drin, and the Lachva. Silver mines were wrought in this country before the arrival of the Turks, but they have all been neglected since. An ore containing quicksilver is found near the convent of Krissevo; and a lead mine was discovered near Olovo, between Kladain and Varish. Population.] The population of B. has been variously estimated: by Hassel it is stated at 560,000; by the editors of the Dictionnaire Geographique at 822,000, of whom 470,000 were represented to be Mussulmans, 190,000 Greeks, 150,000 Catholics, 2,000 Jews, and the remainder Zingarians or Gypsies. By Von Zedlitz the pop. was estimated, in 1828, at 743,000, of whom 250,000 were Bosniaks, 240,000 Turks, 120,000 Servians, 75,000 Morlachians, 40,000 Croats, 16,000 Gypsies, 2,000 Jews, and 800 Armenians. A more recent estimate carries the pop. to 900,000, of whom 500,000 are Mahommedans, 200,000 Greeks, and 150,000 Catholics. The natives are a brave and warlike people, and, with the Albanians, make the best soldiers in the Turkish empire. Government, &c.] B. is under the government of a pasha of three tails, who resides at Traunik, and who is generally changed every three years. Under him are 6 pashas of two tails, who govern so many districts, namely, Traunik, Bagnalouka, or Banyaluka, Srebernik, Isvornik, Kersek, and Rascia or Novibazar. It is subdivided into 48 circles, each having its own cap., in which resides a military officer, and a cadi. Each of these head towns has a kulla or with cannon. But these are not the only defences of the country; for Bihacz, Bagnalouka, Zwornik, and other cities, are important fortresses, as the Austrians have more than once found to their cost. History.] B. in ancient times was denominated Pannonia Inferior; and, in the Middle ages, had its own governors, who were vassals of the Hungarian sovereigns. In 1463, Mohammed the Great subdued Bosnia, and flayed alive its last sovereign, Stephen V. The modern pashalic is of much greater extent than the ancient kingdom of Bosnia, which corresponded to Northern or Lower Bosnia, bounded by the Verbas on the W, and the Drin on the E, but which now includes Turkish Croatia, between the Verbas, the Unna, and the Koranna. Upper Bosnia comprehends all the tract S of the Dinaric mountains, formerly called Herzegovina, Rama, or Duchy of St. Saba, and sometimes Turkish Dalmatia.

BOSOST, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 80 m. N of Lerida, on the l. bank of the Garonne, in the Val-de-Aran. BO'SPORUS,—commonly but incorrectly written Bosphorus, -the BOGHAZ of the Turks, and Bosporus Thracius of the ancients, a strait about 20 m. in length which connects the Black sea with the sea of Marmora, and divides Europe from Asia. At its entrance from the latter sea, between the Seven towers (a), where the walls of Constantinople commence, to the hamlet of Kadi Koi (b) on the Asiatic side, it is <math>4\frac{1}{9}</math> nautical miles in breadth, with soundings in mid-channel of from 18 to 24 fath.; but it rapidly contracts, the coast on the side of the city trending E by N to the Seraglio point (c), a distance of 5 m.; while on the opposite side it trends N by W to Scutari (d). The strait between these two points does not exceed 1 m. in breadth. Turning the Seraglio point, a deep inlet (e e) runs off towards the NW between the city of Constantinople Proper (C) on the S, and the suburbs of Tophana and Galata (D) on the N, and, rising above them Pera, the residence of the Franks, this inlet is the harbour of Constantinople, called Auricornu or Golden-horn in the days of Pliny. At its entrance this inlet is about 600 paces broad; but it becomes more and more contracted as it extends inland 6 or 8 m., and at length terminates in a bend towards the NE, at a point where a rivulet empties itself into it, and where a pleasing mall, or promenade, for the suburb of Pera, has been formed. "A forest of masts, and the length of the port, give it the appearance of the Thames near London; and we were also reminded of the entrance to the great canal of Venice by the number and peculiar form of the boats, which are seen gliding swiftly through the water in all directions." [Marmont.] The first bend or reach of the B. from the harbour, proceeding towards the Black sea, extends 3 m. ENE, with an average breadth of about m., and a depth of from 20 to 32 fath. mid-channel. On the European side are the villages of Fondokli (f), Dolma Bakshi (g), and Orta (h); and on the Asiatic, Scutari (d), off the point of which stands the tower and battery of Leander (i), and the villages of Kus Gundjuk (l), Istavros (m), and Beglerbeg (n). The next bend trends irregularly 5 m. NE by N, and contracts between two castles, one on each side (at o o) to a breadth of only m., while the depth varies from 30 to 40 fath. At this spotwhich is the part of the channel where the Persians, Goths, Latins, and Ottomans, successively crossed it-"the B. appears like a majestic river, winding between banks high and woody as those of the Wye, and not less lively and cultivated than those of the Thames." Opposite Yeniskoi (p), on the European coast, it trends NE, 21 m., to the gulf of Buyukdéré (q), whence it trends NE by E, 4 m., with a breadth varying from \( \frac{3}{4} \) m. to 1\( \frac{1}{2} \) m. to the Black sea, which it enters between two forts and two lighthouses, those on the Asiatic side <math>(r)</math> bearing SE by E, 2 m. from those on the European (s). The deceptive appearance of the coast both to the E and W of the entrance of the B. from the Black sea has often proved fatal to mariners; and the lighthouses are so illmanaged as to be nearly useless. The depth of this last reach is from 20 to 50 fath.; midway between the two lighthouses it is from 34 to 39 fath. The general appearance and some of the geological features of this channel are touched upon in our article on the BLACK SEA. The strong current which sets in from the Black sea into this strait, running at the rate of from 35 to 4 m. an hour, is opposed and deflected in an irregular manner by the abrupt angular bends of the coast, which at the same time convert it in appearance into a series of lovely inland lakes, of an intense blue, the shores of which open into deep bays running up into valleys richly clothed with Oriental foliage, or are covered with the kiosks of the wealthy Frank, Greek, and Armenian merchants of Constantinople, planted amidst orange groves, orchards, and vineyards. The European side, from Constantinople, until within 4 or 5 m. of the Black sea, is almost uninterruptedly studded with fanciful and ornamental buildings, and smiling villages; while the background is a succession of terraced gardens, rising up the side of a hilly range whose graceful undulations are crowned with groves of plane, lime, cypress, walnut, and chestnut. On the Asiatic side, the hills rise into mountains, and the groves swell into forests; and Mount Olympus is seen in the far distance towering over the beautiful town of Brussa. The shores of the B. for some miles from the Black sea, are, on either side, rugged and precipitous, presenting abrupt cliffs and rocky headlands. The shores of the Black sea, on arriving at the entrance of the strait, sweep off at once on either hand nearly at right angles, BOS presenting a vast and sudden expanse of dark waters with most imposing effect after the confined scenery of the strait. The B. is merely defended by open batteries, which though well-placed for opposing shipping, are commanded by heights immediately in their rear.—The name Bosporus literary signifies 'Ox ford; and, according to mythological tradition, this strait was so named from the passage of Io across it when transformed into a heiter. Dr. Neale supposes the strait may have derived its name from the passage of the Persian elephants which the Greeks and Thracians called bos leucos, i. e., 'White bulls.'-Sandys.-Tournefort.—E. D. Clarke's Travels.—Hobhouse. Letter xlv.—Andreossy, Constantinople et le Bosphore. 1828. 8vo.

BOSPORUS (CIMMERIAN). See KAFFA. BOSRA, Bostra, or Busrah, a town of Syria, cap. of the Hauran, in the pash, and 60 m. S of Damascus, and 90 m. NE of Jerusalem, on the Nahrel-Ghazal, a torrent which descends from the Kelb Hauran, and which is crossed by a fine ancient bridge. Burckhardt places it in N lat. 32° 40'. This town is now chiefly remarkable for the magnificent remains which it still exhibits of its former greatness. Of these the principal are, the strong citadel built by the Saracens, the ancient walls, a triumphal arch, and the Deir-Bohaira, or the house of the Nestorian monk Bohaira, who first acknowledged the prophetic character of Mahomet. The modern buildings are, to a great extent, constructed of materials taken from the surrounding ruins, and are sometimes conjoined with ancient edifices. The colonnade of a temple and a long bridge now form the approach to a castle which occupies the site of an old Roman theatre. The streets are narrow. There are several well-built bazaars and mosques. B. is of great antiquity, and was, from a remote period, a town of importance. It was the Roman cap. of Palestina Tertia, or Arabia Provincia.
BOSSAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre and Loire, cant. of Preuilly. Pop. 1,691. It contains some iron-works.
BOSSE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Le Coudray-St.-Germer, 12 m. SW of Beauvais. Pop. 797.
BOSSEI, a town of Switzerland, on the S frontiers of the cant. of Vaud, 4 m. S of Geneva.
BOSSEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, NW of Frankfort. SOPHIA, FROM THE BOSPHORI A Billarton & C. London & Edinburgh. (Constantinople, TOTAL PROPERTY. . -6 4. 14.
BOSSERNE, an island of Denmark, on the N extremity of the Great Belt, to the E of Samsoe island.
BOSSIERE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 12 m. NW of Namur. Pop. 528. In the environs, at the hamlet of Golzinne, are quarries of fine black marble.
BOSSILIEVO, or Bosiljevo, a village of Austrian Croatia, 12 m. WSW of Carlstadt. In the environs is an extensive quarry of fine blue marble.
BOSSINEY, a village in the p. of Tintagel, in Cornwall. Pop. 87.
BOSSINGTON, a chapelry in the p. of Broughton, in Hampshire, 3 m. SW by S of Stockbridge. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 60.
BOSSOLASCO, a village of Piedmont, in the division of Coni, in the prov. and 12 m. SSE of Alba. Pop. 1,500.
BOSSUS-LES-WALCOURT, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, cant. of Beaumont, 14 m. S of Charleroi. It contains an iron-finery. The Austrians were defeated by the French here in 1792.
BOSSUT (CAPE), a promontory of Western Australia, on the coast of Tasman land, forming the S side of Lagrange bay, in S lat. 18° 42', E long. 121° 45' [Stokes]. It is faced by the Casuarina reef.

BOSSUTH, a river of Austria, in Slavonia, which takes its rise in the Gillgebirge, to the N of Brod, and runs to the E, under the name of Bigi. At Tzerna, where it takes the appellation of B., it divides into two branches, both of which fall into the Save, the first 7 m. S of the point of separation, and the other, after a circuitous course to the SE of upwards of 48 m., at the village of Bossuth, 12 m. W of Mitrovitz. This river is the ancient Bacuntius.
BOST, or KILA-BEAST, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Seistan, on the l. bank of the Helmund, to the SW of the junction of the Turnuk, and 80 m. WSW of Kandahar.

BOSTAM, a town of Persia, in Khorasan, 3½ m. NE of Shahrud, in a valley watered by a stream descending from Elburz. It is enclosed by a wall, furnished with numerous round towers, and is about 1 m. in circuit, but contains little more than ruins and small fields; and has a pop. of only between 300 and 400.—Fraser.
BOSTAN, a town of Nubia, in the prov. of Wady Nuba, on the r. bank of the Nile, opposite Mosmos, 25 m. SW of Derr.

BOSTAN (EL), a town of Turkey in Asia, in the sanjak and 23 m. NNW of Marash, on the l. bank of the Sihun, to the WNW of the Taurus chain, and 115 m. SSW of Sivas. Pop. 9,000. This town, the ancient Comana of Cappadocia, occupies a picturesque situation, in a well-watered and fertile plain; and contains 4 mosques, one of which is supposed to have formerly been a temple of Bellona. Grain forms the chief article of its commerce.
BOSTANUK, a town of Persia, in the sanjak of Irak-Ajemi, 95 m. NE of Hamadan, aud 45 m. SW of Kasbin, on a tributary of the Kara Su.
BOSTITSA. See VONITZA.
BOSTOCK, a township in the p. of Davenham, 3 m. NW of Middlewich in Cheshire. Area 930 acres. Pop. 190.

BOSTON, a parish, borough, port, and markettown, in the co. of Lincoln; 116 m. road-distance N of London, and 36 m. SSE of Lincoln, on the river Witham, which here forms a considerable estuary, though 5 m. from the sea. The Witham flows through the town, and is crossed by an elegant ironbridge of one arch, 86 ft. in span, after a design by Rennie,—erected in 1804-7 by the corporation, at an expense of £22,000. The p. contains 7,923 acres. BOS Pop. in 1801, 5,926; in 1811, 8,180; in 1821, 10,373; in 1831, 11,240; in 1841, 12,942. The town, which is in N lat. 53° 10', W long. 0° 25', is well-built, and contains many good dwellinghouses and shops, and extensive granaries and other warehouses. The church is a spacious and elegant structure. The interior is 382 ft. in length, by 99 ft. in breadth; the nave is rich and lofty; and on each side of the chancel are ranges of stalls. The tower is 262 ft. 9 in. in height, and resembles that of the cathedral of Antwerp. It forms a conspicuous landmark for sailors, being visible at sea for 40 m., and is surmounted by an elegant octagonal lantern which seems designed to have been lighted up at night for a sea-mark. This structure was erected in 1309. The streets are well-paved and lighted with gas; but the supply of water is bad. Wednesday and Saturday are market-days, on which large quantities of poultry are sold, much of which is sent to the London market, and an extensive business is done in corn and wool. A cattle market is held once a-week on an open space near the NE end of the town, called the Wide Bargate. Immense numbers of sheep and horned cattle are sold here. Fairs are held on the 4th and 5th of May for sheep chiefly; August 5th is the town-fair; on November 20th, and three following days, for horses; and on December 11th for cattle. The navigation of the Witham has suffered much from the deposition of silt, but since the middle of the last cent. it has been deepened, and a canal carried from the town to Dogdyke, with most beneficial consequences. The quay is now accessible to vessels of 100 tons burden, and the river is navigable to Lincoln, whence is a vast inland communication by canals communicating with the river Trent. For the improvement of the port and harbour of B. extensive powers are vested by two local acts of parliament, in the mayor and burgesses. The expenditure under these acts, from 1812 to 1832, was £88,749 ls. 10d. In 1832, 174 vessels belonged to this port. No manufactures are carried on in the borough; but the town contains two iron-founderies and 3 building-yards, (with a patent slip,) where vessels of 200 tons burden, and downwards, are constructed. The tide rises 13 ft. in the harbour, but if certain improvements on the great level of the Wash, now contemplated, are carried out, it is expected that 19 ft. of water will be obtained here. The registered shipping of this port on 1st Jan., 1847, was 180 ships = 8,343 tons. The number of vessels that entered inwards in 1845, was <math>1,583 = 72,703</math> tons; outwards, 1,097 = 48,414 tons. The steam-vessels entering inwards in 1845 were 92 = 6,817 tons. The foreign trade of B. is chiefly with the Baltic. The export trade, chiefly in agricultural produce, has increased greatly since the draining of the extensive fens, from which upwards of 70,000 acres of rich marsh land have been obtained. The assessed property of the borough was returned in 1815 at £3,423; in 1842-3 at £54,503.—B. is now connected with the Great Northern line at Peterborough, by what is called the Loop line of railway, from its curling round in the form of a loop from Peterborough, through Spalding, Boston, and Lincoln. Its distance from Peterborough by railway is 32 m.; from Lincoln, 30 m. By the E Lincolnshire railway it is also connected with Louth and Grimsby. B. is a borough by prescription, but is chiefly governed by a charter of the 37° Henry VIII., extended by Elizabeth. By the municipal act of 1835, the borough has been divided into two wards: Boston West ward, and Bargate ward; to each of which 9 councillors have been assigned. The mayor is by virtue of his office, escheater, returning officer at the election of members of parliament for the borough, and clerk-of-the-market. A commission of the peace has been granted to this town. The elective franchise was conferred in the reign of Edward VI., from which period B. returned two members to parliament. Under the reform bill the old borough of B., with the parish of Skirbeck and hamlet of Skirbeck quarter, exclusive of the fen-allotment of the p. of Skirbeck, returns two members to parliament. The pop. of the parl. borough in 1841 was 14,618. The electors in 1839-40 were 1,086; in 1842-3, 1,127. B. is also a polling-place for the members for the parts of Kesteven. - John Foxe, the biographer of the Martyrs, was born here; also the ecclesiastical writer, John of Boston, sometimes called Boston of Bury. The Irby family hold the title of Viscount from this town.—The B. poor-law union comprehends 27 parishes, embracing an area of 168 sq. m.; with a pop. returned in 1831 at 29,941. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district, during the three years preceding the formation of the union, was £16,705. Expenditure in 1838, £10,161; in 1841, £10,302; in 1846, £11,995. The name is supposed by some to be a contraction of Botolph'stown; a monastery having been founded here in 650, by the Saxon, St. Botolph. In the 'Ordinacio Stapularum' of Edward III., it is termed St. Botolph. Lambard writes: "St. Botulphes, now Bostonstow. A good towne in Lincolnshyre, commonly and corruptlye called Boston." Having been destroyed by the Danes in 870, the monastery was rebuilt to the N of the church, and its remains are still visible in a dwelling-house, called Botolph's priory. Dr. Stukeley supposes that a Roman ferry crossed the river Witham, in the immediate vicinity of this town. In 1204, Boston paid £780 of duty, while London, under the same tax, paid only £836; it must, therefore, have ranked pretty near in commercial importance to the metropolis itself. The town suffered greatly by fire-the work of an incendiary-in the reign of Edward L; and, in 1285, from a great inundation, so that its prosperity was on the decline, when, in the 27° of Edward III., it was made a staple for wools, leather, tin, lead, and other commodities. The Hanseatic merchants also having settled a guild here, its prosperity revived; and in the reign of Edward III. B. was able to supply 16 vessels to an armament intended for the invasion of Brittany. When Leland wrote in 1530, its decay as a place of commerce had recommenced; and notwithstanding the patronage of Henry and Elizabeth, it gradually declined with the increasing difficulty of the Witham navigation.

BOSTON, the cap. of Massachusetts, the metropolis of New England, and the second commercial city in the American Union, situated mostly on a peninsula 3 m. long, and little more than 1 m. broad, at the head of Massachusetts bay, and possesses one of the best harbours in the United States. It is in 42° 21' 23" N lat., and 71° 4' 9" W long. from Greenwich; and 5° 11′ 15" E long. from Washington. It is 105 m. from Portland on Maine, 214 m. from New York, 302 m. from Philadelphia, 439 m. from Washington, 985 m. from Cincinnati, and 1,809 m. from New Orleans.—The pop. in 1790 was 18,033; in 1800, 24,937; in 1810, 33,250; in 1820, 43,298; in 1830, 61,391; in 1840, 93,338; in 1845, 114,366. B. consists of three parts, viz., B. on the peninsula, or Old B., South B., formerly a part of Dorchester, but united to B. in 1804,—and East B., formerly Noddle's island. The Neck or isthmus, which in early times formed the only connection of the peninsula with the main land, is over a mile in length, and still constitutes the main avenue to the city from the S; but by a number of extension bridges and artificial avenues, it is connected in various directions with the surrounding country. By the fortification of this neck, at the commencement of the Revolutionary war, the British troops were able to control the intercourse between B. and the adjacent country; but communication is now opened in various directions by a number of bridges. Charles river bridge, 1,503 ft. long, connects B. with Charlestown; West B. bridge, 2,758 ft. in length, with a causeway 3,432 ft. long, leads to Cambridge; South B. bridge, 1,550 ft. long, leads from the Neck to S Boston; Canal bridge, bridge, 1,828 ft., connects Old B. with South B.; Warren bridge, 1,390 ft., leads to Charlestown; the Western avenue, 13 m. long, leads to Brooklyn, and constitutes a tidal dam enclosing a pond of 600 acres, which affords an important water-power. The city exhibits a picturesque and beautiful appearance when approached from the sea; and in surveying its several parts the traveller finds much to admire. The peninsula had originally an uneven surface, and the place early received the name of Trimountain, from its three hills, and their principal eminences on Beacon hill. These eminences, though somewhat lowered, continue to adorn the city. Beacon hill, on the W part, is 110 ft. above high-water mark; it was originally 30 ft. higher; Fort hill, on the E side, fronting the harbour, is 80 ft. high; and Copp's hill, in the N part, is 50 ft. above high water. South B. extends about 2 m. along the S side of the harbour. Nearly in the middle of this tract are Dorchester heights, or Mount Washington, 130 ft. above the sea. East B., built on Noddle's island, is connected with the city by a steam-ferry, and with Chelsea, on the main land, by a bridge 600 ft. long. The Eastern railroad commences here. A wharf 1,000 ft. long, called Cunard's wharf, has been granted free of charge for 20 years, for the use of the Liverpool line of steam-ships. Long wharf is 1,650 ft. long; and Central wharf 1,240 ft. East B. has wholly grown up since 1833, and already constitutes an important part of the city. The Common, on the declivity of Beacon hill, has a pleasantly diversified surface, and covers a space of 75 acres. The Statehouse occupies its N part. B. is generally well-built, mostly of brick, and many of its private residences are elegant. Among the public buildings, the State-house is the principal. It is situated on the summit of Beacon hill, 110 ft. above the level of the sea, and fronts on the spacious Common. This edifice is 173 ft. long, and 61 ft. wide. The lower story has a large hall or public walk in the centre, 50 ft. square, and 20 ft. high, supported by Doric columns. In the middle of the S side of this story, is a statue of Washington, by Chantry. Above are the representatives' room, in the centre, 55 ft. square; and the senate-chamber, 55 ft. long, 33 ft. wide, and 30 ft. high, with two screens of Ionic columns, supporting with their entablature a richly-decorated arched ceiling. In another part of the building is the council-chamber, 27 ft. square. The dome is ascended by a spiral stairway on the inside; and from its top is obtained a view of the harbour, the bay, and the surrounding country.-Faneuil hall was erected in 1742, by a gentleman whose name it bears, and was by him presented to the city. It was enlarged in 1805, and is now 100 ft. long, 80 ft. wide, and three stories high. The lower story is occupied by stores. The great hall in the second story is 76 ft. square, and 28 ft. high, with galleries on three sides, supported by Doric columns; the ceiling is supported by two ranges of Ionic columns. The W end is ornamented by a full-length portrait of Washington by Stuart; and another of Peter Faneuil, Esq. Above the great hall, is another, in the third story, 78 ft. long, and 30 ft. wide, devoted to military exercises. This venerable building has been appropriately called "the cradle of American liberty." It is the property of the city, and secured by its charter from ever being devoted to any but public purposes. There are 75 churches in the city. The old Statehouse, at the head of State-street, was built in 1658; it has been twice destroyed by fire, and was last rebuilt in 1747. It was formerly occupied by the statelegislature. An elegant Exchange has been recently erected in State-street. It contains the post-office, a 2,796 ft. in length, leads to E Cambridge; Free reading-room, &c. Quincy-market is a splendid edifice, 585 ft. long, and 50 ft. wide. The wings are two stories high, and at the E and W ends are five porticoes of four Grecian Doric columns. The new court-house, constructed of hewn granite, is 176 ft. long, 54 ft. wide, and 57 ft. high; and the N and S fronts are adorned with Grecian Doric porticoes. The interior has four court-rooms, each 50 by 40 ft., besides various public offices. The custom-house has an elegant Doric portico its whole length, and a fine dome in the centre. The houses of industry, correction, and reformation, are pleasantly situated in South B., near the brow of Dorchester-heights, and are surrounded by ornamented grounds. The Tremont-house is one of the finest hotels in the United States. There are two theatres in the city. Boston has 106 literary and charitable societies. The Medical institution of Harvard university is located in B., where its professors reside. It was founded in 1782, has 6 professors, 157 students; and 5,000 volumes in its library. The lectures commence on the first Wednesday of November, annually.-The institution for the Blind has been liberally patronized; it has a splendid edifice on Mount Washington, South B.—Among the literary societies of a high order, is the American academy of Arts and Sciences, founded in 1780, which has published 4 quarto volumes of its transactions, and has a library of 2,000 vols.-The Massachusetts historical society was incorporated in 1794. Its collections have been published in 26 vols. 8vo., in which are many valuable papers. Its library, consisting of books and manuscripts, amounts to 6,000 articles, many of them rare and valuable, besides several paintings of distinguished individuals.—The B. Athenæum was incorporated in 1837, and has a library of 30,000 vols., a collection of 2,000 coins and medals, and a reading - room well - furnished with American and foreign newspapers.—The Boston society of Natural history was instituted in 1830, and has a cabinet and museum.—The Boston museum possesses a rare and valuable collection of curiosities. —The Massachusetts hospital, in the W part of the city, is deserving of notice. It is a beautiful edifice, built of granite, and surrounded by grounds highly ornamented with trees and shrubbery. There are 36 newspapers published here, of which 12 are daily; of the remainder, a portion are semi-weekly and weekly, and others weekly. Besides newspapers, there are a number of magazines and reviews; the most distinguished of the latter is the North American review. -The city is supplied with water brought from Cochituate lake, 20 m. distant. See article Cochituate. -The harbour extends from Nantasket to the city, and spreads from Nahant to Hingham, and contains 75 sq. m. It is defended by Forts Warren and Independence, the former of which is on Governor's island, and the latter on Castle island. The inner harbour has a depth of water sufficient for 500 vessels of the largest class to ride at anchor in safety, while the entrance is so narrow as scarcely to admit two ships abreast. B. in point of commerce is the second town in the Union. Its wealth and enterprise have long been actively employed in foreign commerce, to which its noble harbour has materially contributed. Several large steamships form an important packet-line between B. and Great Britain. This line has generally performed its voyage to Liverpool, touching at Halifax, in 121 days. The distance, by Mercator's sailing,— From Boston dock to Liverpool dock, is The distances by the Great circle are expressed in the 2d col.—The Asia made the voyage from Boston to Liverpool, between June 12th and 22d, 1850, in 9 d. 18 h. 40 m.—Lines of Packets sail from B. to every port of importance in the Union. By means of the Middlesex canal, which extends to the Merrimac, it has a boatable communication with Concord in New Hampshire; and a railroad communication exists with Portland, on the NE, 105 m.; Concord, on the N, 76 m.; Fitchburg, on the NW, 50 m.; Albany, NW by W, 200 m., and Hudson, WNW, 193 m.; New York, WSW, 220 m., and Brooklyn, 215 m.; Allen's Point, SW by W, 110 m.; Stonington, SW, 90 m.; New Bedford, S, 31 m.; Plymouth, S by E, 37 m.—The capitalists of B. are large proprietors in the manufacturing establishments at Waltham and at Lowell. -The tonnage of B., in 1840, was 220,243 tons. The imports were valued at about 16,000,000 dollars; the exports at 10,000,000. There were, in 1840, 142 commercial houses in B, and 89 commission houses, engaged in foreign trade, with a cap. of 11,676,000 dollars; 572 retail-stores, with a cap. of 4,184,220 dollars; 31 lumber-yards, with a cap. of 371,000 dollars. The remaining capital was chiefly invested in fisheries, machinery, furnaces, distilleries, paint works, glass-factories, sugar-refineries, rope-walks, and printing offices.—A singular branch of commerce existing here is that of the Ice-trade, as it is called. This trade was begun in 1805 by Frederick Tudor, of B. The first enterprise resulted in a loss of about 4,500 dollars; but Mr. Tudor, nevertheless, followed up the trade, until the embargo and war put a stop to foreign trade. Its operations up to this period had been confined to Martinique and Jamaica. After the close of the war in 1815, Mr. Tudor recommenced his operations by shipments to Havannah, under a contract with the government of Cuba. He extended it, in 1817, to Charlestown; in the following year to Savannah; and in 1820 to New Orleans. On the 18th of May, 1833, the first shipment of ice was made to the East Indies, by Mr. Tudor, in the ship Tuscany, for Calcutta, and since that period he has extended his operations to Madras and Bombay. Previously to 1832, the shipments had been small, the whole amounting, in 1832, to 4,352 tons, which was taken entirely from Fresh Pond, in Cambridge, and shipped by Mr. Tudor, who was then alone in the trade: since 1832, the trade has increased much, and appears destined to a still more rapid increase for some years. It has also been divided among many parties, and its methods have been improved, and a knowledge of them more widely diffused. The ice has been chiefly taken from Fresh and Spy ponds; and since 1841, mainly transported on the Charlestown branch-railroad, which was constructed for that purpose. Recently, ice establishments have been made at most of the ponds near B., and it is probable that in a few years the product of all these waters may be required to supply the trade. The shipments of ice from B. coastwise, for the year ending December 31, 1847, amounted to 51,887 tons, in 258 vessels; the shipments to foreign ports during the same period were 22,591 tons, in 95 vessels, to ports in the East and West Indies, Hong Kong, and Liverpool. The freight paid on the ice shipped abroad and coastwise is estimated at 186,196 dollars; the cost of securing the ice and stowing it on board vessels, at an average of 2 dols. per ton, 148,956 dols. There were, during the year, upwards of 29 cargoes of provisions, fruit, and vegetables shipped in ice, to southern ports, where otherwise such articles could not be sent, the value of which is estimated at 72,500 dols.; profit on the where profitable return freights cannot be obtained, because to such places a heavy freight must be paid on the ice, which it cannot bear; and also because southern places, which do not produce valuable ex ports, are usually unable to consume expensive luxuries. At the present time sawdust is used almost exclusively for preserving the ice during voyages of considerable length. It is placed immediately between the ice and the sides of the vessel. The prices at which ice sells in places where there is competition vary constantly. In Havannah, where it is a monopoly, it is sold at 64 cents per pound, and there the trade has not increased since 1832, when the shipments were 1,112 tons, while at New Orleans, where it has been sold at from half a cent to three cents per pound, it has increased during the same period from 2,310 to upwards of 28,000. At Calcutta the trade commenced in 1833, with a shipment for that year of 201 tons, and the price has never been above six cents per pound, and is now about 25 cents. The exports to that place had increased in 1847 to 3,000 tons, but probably less than onefifth of that quantity is actually sold, owing to the great length of the voyage. The consumption of ice in B. and the vicinity during the year 1847 was 27,000 tons, at an estimated cost of 54,795 dols. It was sold at an average price of 13\frac{1}{2} cents per 100 pounds, of 72,800 dols. for the 27,000 tons; leaving a profit of 18,135 dols. to be divided amongst the seven principal ice-dealers. Ice being shipped and used at all seasons, large storehouses are required to preserve it. Exclusive of icehouses on the wharves at Charlestown and East Boston, in which ice is stowed for short periods, there had been erected, in 1847, and previously, houses capable of containing 141,332 tons. B. was founded in August, 1630. In the reign of Charles II., the inhabitants of the colony fell under the royal displeasure, and, in 1683, a writ of quo warranto was issued against the charter. A legal town-meeting of the freemen of B. was held, and the question was put to vote, whether it was their wish that the general court should resign the charter and the privileges therein granted, when it was resolved in the negative unanimously. The charter, however, was declared forfeited by a decree of the courtof-chancery; and, soon after, Sir Edmond Andros was appointed the first royal governor. His administration, which endured for two or three years, was arbitrary and oppressive. In April, 1689, the people of B. took forcible possession of the fort and the castle in the harbour, turned the guns upon the frigate Rose, and compelled her to surrender, seized the governor, and held him a close prisoner under guard in the castle. A little more than a month afterwards, news was received of the Revolution in England, and the event was celebrated with great rejoicings. In 1765, after the passage of the stamp-act, the person appointed distributorof-stamps was compelled, by threats of violence, to decline the acceptance of the office, and the house of the lieutenant-governor was destroyed by a mob. A large military and naval force was stationed at B. for the purpose of overawing the people. On the evening of March 5, 1770, a sergeant's guard fired upon a crowd of people, who were surrounding them and pelting them with snow-balls, and killed five men. Dec. 16, 1773, on the arrival of three ships loaded with tea, after various unsuccessful attempts had been made by public meetings of the citizens, to prevent its being landed and sold, in violation of the non-importation resolves of the people, a number of men, disguised as Indians, went on board the ships, and threw all the tea overboard. In the following spring, the port of B. was closed by an act of parliament -the Boston Port-bill-and the landing and shipping of goods within the harbour was ordered to be discontinued. The session of the general court was removed to Salem, and additional bodies of troops and a military governor were ordered to B. In 1775, the Revolutionary war commenced with the battles of Lexington and Bunker-hill; and the town of B, in which the British troops were encamped to the number of 10,000 men, was besieged by the American army. The siege continued until the March following, when the troops evacuated the town and castle, embarked on board their own ships, and withdrew to another part of the country. The inhabitants of B. were among the earliest and most ardent asserters of the rights of the people, and active supporters of independence. During the Revolutionary struggle, popular meetings were frequently held in Faneuil hall. Benjamin Franklin was born in B., Jan. 17, 1706.

BOSTON, a township of Erie co., in the state of New York, U. S., 18 m. SW of Buffalo. It presents a hilly surface, drained by Canquaga creek; and the soil consists of clay and gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,745. The village is situated on Canquaga creek. Pop. 500.—Also a township of Summit co., in the state of Ohio, 139 m. NE of Columbus, on the Ohio canal. It is intersected by the Cuyahoga, which BOT affords good water power. The soil is fertile, and is extensively covered with white oak. Pop. 815. The village of the same name consists of about 20 houses. Another, called Boston Centre or Peninsula, containing 25 dwellings, is situated in the centre of the township, at the aqueduct of the Ohio canal over the Cuyahoga.—Also a township of Ionia co., in the state of Michigan, 146 m. NW of Detroit, on the Grand river. Pop. 85.—Also a township of Franklin co., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. 224.
BOSTON, a town of Guinea, in the delta of the Ponga river, 110 m. WNW of Furicaria.
BOSTRA. See Bosra.
BOSTRUP, a village of Denmark, on the N coast of the island of Langeland, 10 m. N of Rudkioping.

BOSWELL'S (St.), a parish in Roxburghshire, on the S bank of the Tweed. Area of p. 2,200 acres. Pop. 747, of whom 399 were in the v. of Lessudden. The Edinburgh and Hawick railway has a station here 40 m. from Edinburgh, and 7 m. from Galashiels. The largest sheep and cattle fair in the S of Scotland is held here on the 18th of July.
BOSWILLER. See BOUXWILLER.
BOSWORTH. See MARKET-BOSWORTH.
BOSWYL, or Bozwyl, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Argau, on a tributary of the Aar, 16 m. ESE of Aarau.
BOSYT (CAPE), or RAS-EL-BASIT, a promontory on the coast of Syria, in the pashalik of Aleppo, in N lat. 35° 53', and E long. 35° 47'.
BOSYT, or Bosseda, a village of Syria, in the pashalik and 77 m. WSW of Aleppo, at the entrance of a small stream into the Mediterranean, 9 m. ENE of Cape Bosyt.

BOSZORMENY, a town of Hungary, cap. of the district of the Heiduks, in the comitat of Szabolcs, 12 m. NNW of Debrecin, and 9 m. S of Dorog. Pop. 14,660. It is large and well built, and contains a Greek and a Reformed church.
BOSZORMENY, a town of Hungary, near the N bank of the Sebes koros, 12 m. WNW of Grosswardein.
BOT, a river of S Africa, which separates the SE side of the co. of Stellenboch from the co. of Zwellendam, and flows into Sandown bay, 24 m. SW of Caledon.

BOTANY BAY, a spacious bay on the E coast of Australia, or of New South Wales, in S lat. 34°, discovered by Captain Cook in his first voyage, in 1770, and so named by him from the number of new plants which the naturalists who accompanied him found on its shores. On Captain Cook's report, the British government resolved to found a settlement at this part of the coast for the reception of criminals sentenced to transportation; and on the 18th of January, 1788, the advance ship of an expedi tion under Captain Arthur Phillip, R. N., consisting of 11 vessels, 6 of which were transports, with 600 male convicts, and 250 female convicts, arrived in B, bay. It was immediately found, however, that this bay, though extensive, did not afford shelter from E winds; and that, in consequence of its shallowness, ships even of moderate draught would always be obliged to anchor with the entrance of the bay open, and thus be exposed to a heavy sea when blowing hard from E. Some parts of the low lands near the coast were a perfect swamp; and the supply of fresh water was very scanty. Captain Phillip—who bore the commission of captain-general and commander-in-chief over the territory of New South Wales—was induced by these circumstances to turn his attention to Port Jackson, a few miles to the N of B. bay, and here he "had the satisfaction to find one of the finest harbours in the world, in which 1,000 sail-of-the-line might ride in perfect security." On a cove on the S side of the harbour, 5 or 6 m. from its entrance, about 1 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth, in which ships could anchor close to shore, the governor bestowed the name of Sydney cove, in honour of Lord Sydney; and the head of it he selected for the site of the seat of government. At this period the coast-line between B. bay and Sydney cove was occupied by a forest, in some parts 11 m., in others 3 m. across; beyond that was a swampy, sandy heath; and beyond that again, to the furthest verge of the horizon, the country appeared covered with trees. It thus appears that the B. bay of such bad notoriety was not in fact Captain Cook's discovery of that name; but Port Jackson. The country around the former bay has only assumed the appearance of cultivation and settlement within these few years. It may be interesting, however, to note the progress of what still continued to be known as the settlement of B. bay. For some time after the original establishment of the settlement, the whole of the convict-population, with the exception of a few retained as domestic servants by the officers of government, were employed, on account of the government, either in agriculture or in the public works; and by new transportations from Britain they received constant accessions. Portions of ground were allotted by government for cultivation, while those who had no means of subsistence were supplied from the public stores; live stock, gradually introduced, was preserved with the utmost care; and the colony speedily began to exhibit a picture of industry. In process of time, free emigrants began to arrive; while some who had originally been landed as convicts, became free by servitude. In this state of things it was usual to assign one or more convicts as servants to such colonists as were able to employ and maintain them; while many convicts obtained for good conduct tickets of exemption from government labour, and liberty to employ themselves for their own advantage. As population and the means of the colony increased, larger portions of land were cleared, and new establishments founded; and an extensive garden was formed, wherein both indigenous and exotic plants were cultivated, and whence many rare and curious vegetables have been transmitted to the mother-country. Sometimes, however, the course of this prosperity was interrupted by the profligate conduct of the convicts, whom neither punishment nor lenity could reclaim: robberies and atrocious murders alarmed the more peaceable part of the community, and the public buildings were in some cases maliciously set on fire. Floods and tempests also occasioned great devastation among the articles of subsistence; and the settlers had long to struggle with all the difficulties of an infantcolony partly dependent on external supplies. Notwithstanding these disasters, which were alleviated by the prudence of the government, some of the colonists, in process of time, declared that they could maintain themselves from their own resources, without any aid from the public stores; and many convicts, abandoning their dissolute habits, willingly lent their aid towards the general welfare. In 1795 the stock of the colony consisted of 57 horses, 227 cattle, 1,531 sheep, 1,427 goats, 1,869 hogs; and 5,419 acres of land were under cultivation. In 1804 the settlers amounted to 7,410, whose stock consisted of 450 horses, 3,500 cattle, 16,500 sheep, and 14,000 swine, and the cultivated lands extended to 11,000 acres. In the year 1810 the total number of individuals belonging to B. bay and its dependencies, amounted to 11,952, of whom 1,100 were military. Of this number there were in New Holland 5,513 men, 2,220 women, 2,721 children: total 10,454. There were above 21,000 acres in cultivation; and the stock amounted to 1,114 horses, 11,276 cattle, 33,818 sheep, 1,732 goats, and 18,992 hogs. Free settlers, on emigrating to the colony, were entitled to have the passage of themselves and families gratis: and, on arrival, had grants of land of 150 acres, if married, and 10 acres for each child, also utensils for clearing the land, and grain for sowing. The expense of the settlement to government for the year 1810 amounted to £72,000. The further progress of the colony is related under the head Australia and New South Wales. The present notice is confined to the history of the settlement as a place of transportation. Nothing can be more interesting than to trace the progress of the settlement of B. bay, formed originally almost exclusively of criminals. The expediency of preserving this settlement, however, as a penal colony, was often doubted, because, notwithstanding its progress and prosperity, its site was chosen under comparative ignorance of the soil and climate, and it has been always attended with a very considerable expense to the mother-country. The distance of the colony, the length of the voyage, and the charges of transporting criminals, were the principal arguments urged against it. Immediately on conviction in Britain, felons were sent to the hulks; and when these were full, a selection was made of all those sentenced to transportation for life, or for 14 years, under 50 years of age, and next among those sentenced to transportation for 7 years, who had been guilty of the most atrocious offences, or proved themselves the most unruly. All females were sent, without exception, under 45 years of age. At first, a contract was entered into between government and the owners of vessels, to carry them out; and in order to insure suitable treatment, the master received a gratuity for each convict who landed in the colony. About 200 men or women were generally embarked on board of one vessel, with a guard of 30 men, and 1 officer. Between the years 1795 and 1801, about every tenth convict died, chiefly it is supposed from bad treatment; but from 1801 to 1810, owing to an improved system, not above 1 in 46. The voyage occupied about six months. On reaching the settlement, the character and capacity of each convict were investigated as far as possible; artificers were employed in the service of government; and others, under certain restrictions, were allowed to go at large. The expense of each convict in the service of government was from £30 to £40 yearly; a free labourer might be hired for £70, but he was generally found to do twice as much work as a convict. On the arrival of a vessel, the unmarried men were permitted to choose wives from among the convicts; but no ceremony was performed to rivet the union, and in fact for 30 or 40 years the religious improvement of the convict-population was entirely neglected. The scarcity of women at first occasioned great disorders and immorality in the colony. The period of the 3 sentence being expired, the convict might either return home, or remain in the settlement: in the latter case an unmarried man obtained a grant of 40 acres of land, and husbandry utensils, be- BOT sides which he was victualled during 18 months from the government stores. If married, the grant extended to 50 acres, and 10 additional acres for each child. Independent of the natural expiry of time, the governor had authority to grant absolute or conditional pardons. No provision, however, was made for bringing convicts to their own country on expiration of the sentence; and the facility with which convicts under sentence for the most heinous crimes obtained their freedom became notorious. The educated classes of convicts rose rapidly to eminence and wealth; they became the leading members of the bar, the newspaper editors, the chief merchants, the wealthy landholders of New South Wales. A better system of transportation was ultimately adopted. A restraint was put upon the discretion of the governor to grant pardons, and convicts were restrained from aspiring to certain employments of potential influence in the colony. Different modifications of the whole system of dealing with the convict portion of the population were successively adopted; and at last transportation to New South Wales was altogether discontinued in 1842. The policy of this measure still continues to be warmly questioned by one school of economists, and as warmly defended by another.

BOTANY ISLAND, an island of the S Pacific, in the group of the New Hebrides, off the SE extremity of New Caledonia, in S lat. 22° 50', E long. 167° 15'. It is flat and sandy, and does not exceed 1 m. in diam. It is covered with pines of gigantic growth, and the etoa tree of Otaheite. The island is infested with flat-tailed water-snakes, and fish of a poisonous description have been found in the surrounding sea.
BOTAO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 8 m. NNE of Coimbra.
BOTCHESTON, a hamlet in the p. of Ratby, Leicestershire, 6 m. ENE of Market - Bosworth. Pop. 37.
BOTEGWELL, a township in the p. of Abergele, in Denbighshire. Pop. 113.
BOTENHEIM, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, in the bailiwick and 1 m. S of Brackenheim, on the Zebee. Pop. 880. The environs afford excellent wine.
BOTESDALE, a hamlet in the p. of Redgrave, in Suffolk, 7 m. WNW of Eye. Area of p. 540 acres. Pop. 633.

BOTETOURT, a county in the state of Virginia, U. S., to the W of the Blue ridge. It comprises an area of 1,000 sq. m.; and presents a generally hilly, and in some parts mountainous surface. It is intersected in the S by the Roanoke, and in the centre and N by the head-streams of James river. Pop. in 1830, 16,354; in 1840, 11,679, of whom 8,357 were whites, 2,925 slaves, and 377 free coloured. Cap., Fincastle.
BOTHALL, a parish and v. of Northumberland, 3 m. E of Morpeth, on the N bank of the Wansbeck. Area 15,130 acres. Pop. 800, of whom 249 were in the township of B. demesne.
BOTHAMSALL, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 43 m. NW of Tuxford, on the N bank of the Meden. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 325.

BOTHAS, or BOTHNIAS, a village of S Africa, in the territory of the cape of Good Hope, in the co. and 21 m. SE of Clanwilliam, to the E of the Cedar Bergen.—Also a hill of S Africa, in the co. of Albany, 7 m. NE of Graham's Town.
BOTHELL AND THREAPLAND, a township in the p. of Torpenhow, Cumberland, 7 m. NNE of Cockermouth. Pop. 455.
